questions.text: I think that the ability to evaluate suffering without being consumed by it is what distinguishes sustainable helpers from those who burn out.
I find that I read social situations the way a musician reads a score, perceiving multiple layers at once.
I notice when someone is testing the waters with a comment before committing to an opinion.
I feel that planning for worst-case scenarios is practical, not pessimistic.
I regularly redirect my personal budget toward helping people around me.
I believe that the moral demand of suffering is clearest when it calls for action that is inconvenient, costly, or socially awkward.
I pour my energy into whatever excites me now and trust I'll recharge when needed.
I find that I can be in a noisy, chaotic environment without feeling overstimulated.
I find it energizing to direct a team through a challenging situation.
I hold the view that generosity should come before security.
I would describe my influence style as directive.
I lay out my clothes, pack my bag, or prepare materials the night before.
I feel that the moral pull I experience in the presence of suffering is evidence of a well-functioning conscience.
I experience embarrassment as a searing, full-body flush that takes a long time to dissipate.
I tend to miss social cues that seem obvious to others.
I find it hard to be around conflict because my body reacts so strongly to tension.
I hold the view that groups find focus naturally when everyone's voice is heard.
I believe investing in my own development is one of the most important things I can do.
